### User (2026-03-13 20:36:05)

Ok so here are the results of 146 and of me asking about jaccard:

INC-0146 KEEP — codex/rr-067-phase-transport-k75-refine (8c273d3)

Critical K discovery: allocate_triplet_bins_budget gives kalpha=2 at K=25 AND K=50 — the original "test at K=50" plan would have failed to resolve the bin dilution. K=75 is the first value where kalpha=3.

Confirm results (2 seeds):

Route	rel_diff	Verdict
HOPF_BASE_K25	+31.2% (stable)	Reference
HOPF_BASE_K75	+46.7% (stable)	K-value control
HOPF_TRANS_K75	+65.1% (stable)	CONFIRM PASS
Fiber beats same-K base by +18.4pp (+39% relative). pt_bins=3 confirmed in both seeds.

Stage 4 status: PARTIAL-PASS confirmed at proxy level. Both mechanisms confirmed:

phase4d_hopf (INC-0145): theta_shift on phase angles → 40.7%
phase4d_hopf_transport (INC-0146): Levi-Civita transported alpha → 65.1%
Next decision point: INC-0147 — Stage 4 4-seed finalize at K=75 HOPF_TRANS, or begin Stage 5 (Spectral). Stage 5 is now unblocked by proxy-level Stage 4 evidence.

Good question to raise. Let me look at the implementation of geodesic_knn_jaccard before drawing any conclusions.

Now I need to understand what v and z are when this is called — specifically what two things it's comparing neighbors between:

Now I understand the full picture. Let me check what route_z_ev is:

Read router_proxy_eval.py, lines 620 to 645

Now I have the full picture. Let me also check what route_coordinate returns:

Now let me look at apply_chart and exp_map0 to understand what the function is actually computing before drawing any conclusions:

Good catch by the coordinator. This is an important verification. The answer has a precise mathematical explanation — let me trace the full call chain carefully:

geodesic_knn_jaccard computes:

Take 256 random points from v_ev (raw embeddings) and route_z_ev = apply_chart(v_ev, chart)
Map both through exp_map0(x) = tanh(||x||) * x/||x|| into the Poincaré ball
Compute pairwise Poincaré distances in each space
Find k=8 nearest neighbors; compute Jaccard overlap
apply_chart with learn_scale=0 (all these experiments) is:
w = v @ chart.R.T   # pure orthogonal rotation — that's it
The math proves Jaccard must be 1.0 here:

R is orthogonal, so ||Rv|| = ||v|| for all v
Therefore exp_map0(Rv) = tanh(||v||) * Rv/||v|| = R · exp_map0(v) — the map commutes with rotation
Poincaré distance is rotation-invariant: diff² = ||Rx - Ry||² = ||x-y||², and ||Rx||² = ||x||²
Every pairwise distance is identical in both spaces → all k-NN sets are identical → Jaccard = 1.0 always
This is mathematically guaranteed, not a bug. The metric was designed to detect distortion from learned anisotropic scaling (when learn_scale=1). With learn_scale=0, the chart is a pure isometry — the diagnostic correctly reports 1.0.

The test confirms it. Here is the complete answer for the coordinator:

Jaccard = 1.0 is mathematically correct, not broken. But the diagnostic is inapplicable to these configurations — it carries no health information.

Why 1.0 is guaranteed: The function measures whether the chart transformation preserves hyperbolic nearest-neighbor structure. It compares neighborhoods of v_ev (raw embeddings) vs. route_z_ev = v_ev @ R.T (chart-rotated). Since all experiments run with learn_scale=0, the chart is a pure orthogonal rotation. Orthogonal rotations preserve all pairwise Euclidean and Poincaré distances exactly, so every k-NN set is identical in both spaces → Jaccard = 1.0 always. The test suite even asserts this deliberately.

What the metric was designed for: Detecting distortion when learn_scale=1 is on. If the chart learns anisotropic scaling severe enough to reorder neighbors, Jaccard would drop below 1.0. That's a meaningful signal. With learn_scale=0 it's trivially 1.0.

Implication for INC-0145 / INC-0146: The results remain valid. The Jaccard just wasn't providing any information in these runs — it was never failing, but it also never would have. The pmax discrimination metric (ORIG vs COL_PERM rel_diff) is the actual route health signal in these experiments. If there's a desire for a genuine route health check under learn_scale=0, a different diagnostic is needed — for example, checking that the pmax distribution isn't degenerate, or that shell occupancy is not entirely concentrated in one bucket.

The latest state of the widget is: {"plan": {"plan_id": "plan-2026-03-13T22:10:00Z-hopf-viz", "version": 2, "title": "Hopf vs K-means visualization", "steps": [{"id": "step-1", "text": "Collect INC-0062..INC-0143 artifacts and canonical configs from repo", "status": "in_progress", "reason": "First step started automatically."}, {"id": "step-2", "text": "Survey primary literature on Hopf fibrations, PPMI-SVD, and feature hashing", "status": "pending", "reason": null}, {"id": "step-3", "text": "Reproduce Hopf and k-means projections on the semantic proxy locally", "status": "pending", "reason": null}, {"id": "step-4", "text": "Generate explanatory diagrams and SVGs illustrating Hopf mapping and partitions", "status": "pending", "reason": null}, {"id": "step-5", "text": "Assemble a preprint-style figure set and embed diagrams into the report", "status": "pending", "reason": null}]}, "step_statuses_by_plan": {"plan-2026-03-13T22:10:00Z-hopf-viz": {"step-1": "in_progress", "step-2": "pending", "step-3": "pending", "step-4": "pending", "step-5": "pending"}}, "waiting_for_user_response_on_plan_until": null, "status": "completed", "last_updated_at": "2026-03-14T00:28:01.127454Z", "research_started_at": "2026-03-14T00:26:30.767224Z", "research_stopped_at": "2026-03-14T00:28:01.127193Z", "report_message": {"id": "c9826b61-5179-40b1-ae4e-0588477cd053", "author": {"role": "assistant", "name": null, "metadata": {}}, "create_time": 1773448036.503961, "update_time": null, "content": {"content_type": "text", "parts": ["# Geometric Routing Audit and Stage\u00a03 Analysis\n\n**Executive summary:** The recent project audit (commit **e0af97a** with configs `proxy_transfer_inc0140_angular_sector_screen.json`, `inc0142_confirm_analysis.py`, etc.) found a code bug that invalidated early results: **alpha-bin quantization was dead**, so phase addresses collapsed. After fixing this (all INC-0062..INC-0064 rerun with live bins), the **mechanism proved live** (Hopf/phase steps alter ~98\u201399% of addresses)\u30108\u2020L41-L44\u3011. The wikitext2 proxy\u2019s embedding is an isotropic hash with L2 norm, so it lacks semantic neighborhoods. Thus column-permutation (shuffling each feature) preserves marginal norms but breaks no further structure, making ORIG vs PERM indistinguishable in geometric tests\u301016\u2020L1-L8\u3011\u30100\u2020L0-L3\u3011. In contrast, when we built a **semantic proxy** (PPMI matrix + SVD, analogous to GloVe\u301018\u2020L5-L7\u3011), structure reappeared: INC-0142 saw a +31.8% gap (p_max 0.0860 vs 0.0624) with *z*\u22484.2. INC-0143 (4 seeds) confirmed: ORIG p_max = 0.0905\u00b10.0040, PERM = 0.0613\u00b10.0048, mean shift +0.0292, rel-diff 38.5%, paired *t*(5.8)\u22489.43, *p*\u22481e-4, Cohen\u2019s *d*\u22486.7. The 95% CI for the difference is roughly [\u20130.002, +0.060], narrowly excluding zero.\n\n**Probe validity:** Feature hashing is essentially a random projection\u30100\u2020L0-L3\u3011; normalized random vectors become uniformly spread on the sphere, so no intrinsic clusters. Column-shuffle (permuting each feature across samples) preserves each coordinate\u2019s histogram\u301016\u2020L1-L8\u3011 but destroys covariances. Because the hash proxy had near-zero covariance, PERM \u2248 ORIG under any fixed map. In contrast, semantic embeddings (via PPMI\u2013SVD\u301018\u2020L5-L7\u3011) have high within-class correlations (we observed |corr|\u22480.90 among related dimensions). Using these, the same Hopf routing produced clear ORIG\u2013PERM separation. Thus the Stage\u00a02 \u201cKILL\u201d was a **probe failure**, not a proof that geometry can never read semantics.\n\n**Results detail (INC-0142/0143):**  \n|          | Seed1  | Seed2  | Seed3  | Seed4  | Mean    | StdDev  |\n|----------|--------|--------|--------|--------|---------|---------|\n| p_max(ORIG) | 0.0860 | 0.0888 | 0.0952 | 0.0920 | 0.0905 | 0.0040 |\n| p_max(PERM) | 0.0624 | 0.0652 | 0.0544 | 0.0632 | 0.0613 | 0.0048 |\n| **RelDiff**  | **+41.9%** | **+36.3%** | **+74.1%** | **+45.8%** | **+38.5%** | \u2013 |\n\nPaired *t*-test: *t*(df\u22485.8)=9.43, two-tailed *p*\u22481\u00d710\u207b\u2074; Cohen\u2019s *d*\u22486.7.  95% CI for (ORIG\u2013PERM) is ~[\u20130.002,\u00a0+0.060]. All four seeds exceed +20%. Other metrics: sector entropy drops for ORIG, and Hopf-base error metrics remain tight. The route\u2019s MSE barely changes (0.003900 vs 0.003916), confirming the test detects **structure** rather than model quality.\n\n**Fixed Hopf vs adaptive K-means:** We interpret *fixed H^4 geometry* as a *deterministic 4D projection* (quaternionic Hopf map\u301010\u2020L11-L18\u3011) followed by equal-volume binning. *K-means* (standard Lloyd\u2019s algorithm) is a 100D, data-adaptive vector quantizer\u301013\u2020L3-L8\u3011. In pre-screen one seed: Hopf gave p_max(ORIG)=0.0860, p_max(PERM)=0.0624 (rel_diff +31.8%).  K-means (K=16) gave 0.0668 vs 0.0708 (rel_diff \u20135.8%).  Also, Hopf sectors have tight \u03c7-spread (\u03c3_\u03c7\u22480.058) vs K-means clusters (\u03c3_\u03c7\u22480.246). Both preserve kNN (Jaccard\u22481.0), likely because neither distorts local geometry radically.\n\nThis matches theory: K-means, if refit on each distribution, will produce similarly compact clusters (especially on the permuted data) and erase the difference. A fairer baseline would *freeze* K-means on ORIG and apply to PERM; if that still fails to separate, it confirms that Hopf\u2019s **fixed geometry** captures structural signals K-means ignores. In essence, Hopf is a fixed non-linear kernel that exploits quaternionic symmetry, whereas K-means is a linear Voronoi partitioner.\n\n**Metric definitions and checks:**  \n- *p_max_after* = max bucket fraction post-routing. Uniform 16-way bucket = 0.0625.  \n- *sector_entropy* = entropy of bucket distribution.  \n- *\u03c7_std* = within-bucket std of Hopf\u2019s \u03c7 coordinate (radial component). Small means sectors align radially.  \n- *geodesic_kNN_jaccard* = average Jaccard of k-NN sets pre- vs post-routing (should be <1 if neighbors change). A reported 1.0 for both methods suggests a check bug (neighbors presumably preserved by definition in Hopf\u2019s Riemannian geometry; needs further sanity check). Recommended sanity tests: varying random seed in K-means, checking neighbor recall directly in embedding vs routed space, and verifying column-shuffle preservation of marginals (and breaking covariances, e.g. via covariance matrix measurement\u301016\u2020L1-L8\u3011).\n\n**Threats and reproducibility:** All configs/scripts must be logged. We use commit *e0af97a*, with `configs/proxy_transfer_inc0140_angular_sector_screen.json` (geometry-only, no learning) and `inc0142_confirm_analysis.py` for PPMI\u2013SVD setup. The exact data split and normalization must be fixed in logs. We advise including the random seeds and any train/test split decisions. (In our runs, we followed the user-provided scripts exactly.) Confirm ICC (experimental random variation) by rerunning `proxy_sweep.py` with multiple seeds and with both original/perm proxies. Double-check that no unintended normalization (beyond L2) was applied post-hoc.\n\n**Hardware speedups:** Routing sparseness promises conditional compute gains. In contemporary MoE literature, speedups of ~5\u201310\u00d7 over comparable dense models are reported\u301028\u2020L1-L7\u3011. For example, Switch Transformers (Fedus et al., 2021) and GShard (Lepikhin et al., 2020) demonstrate multi-billion-parameter models with inference throughputs several-fold higher than dense baselines, thanks to sparse expert routing\u301028\u2020L1-L7\u3011. The upper bound of 100\u00d7 comes from extreme assumptions (only 1% of model active per token). Achieving even 30\u00d7 would likely require new hardware or ultra-high sparsity. Our current result (\u223c0.4% MSE gain on proxy) suggests the *inductive bias* helps but real gains depend on system integration. We tentatively assess **2\u201330\u00d7** as a reasonable expected range in real scenarios\u301028\u2020L1-L7\u3011, with 100\u00d7 speculative without co-design.\n\n**Recommended canonical wording:** We suggest the README/state:\n\n> *\u201c**Stage\u00a02:** The Hopf-sector routing law **requires semantically structured input** to generate meaningful partitioning. On normalized hash embeddings (Stage\u00a01 proxy), real vs shuffled data are indistinguishable, so the geometry alone shows no effect. However, on distributional word embeddings (PPMI\u2013SVD or GloVe), the same fixed Hopf geometry **does** separate real data from shuffled control (\u226530% rel. gap in key metric). Thus fixed Hopf routing is validated *as a semantic gate*, not a magic semantic extractor.*\u201d  \n> *\u201c**Stage\u00a03:** The Hopf map implementation and sector partition are correct (confirmed by \u03c7- and neighborhood invariances\u301010\u2020L11-L18\u3011). Intriguingly, this fixed 4D partition captures structure that 100D k-means (adaptive) does not under our test, highlighting the model\u2019s unique inductive bias.*\u201d\n\n**Tables:** (see above) summarize INC-0143 seed runs and Stage\u00a03 pre-screen data.\n\n**Figures:** We include schematic diagrams of the Hopf fibration (S^7\u2192S^4) and a conceptual pipeline. (Images are embedded.) For example:\n\n\u30104\u2020embed_image\u3011 *Figure 1: Hopf fibration conceptual diagram (source: nLab\u301010\u2020L11-L18\u3011)*. It shows S^7 fibers S^3 over base S^4, illustrating the fixed geometry. \n\u30106\u2020embed_image\u3011 *Figure 2: Adaptive vs Fixed Partitioning.* Left: k-means centroids adapt to data clouds. Right: Hopf sectors fixed in 4D space.  \n\u3010??\u2020embed_image\u3011 *Figure 3: Experimental pipeline.* Context windows \u2192 Hash/PPMI\u2013SVD \u2192 (Hopf or k-means) routing \u2192 Metric computation.\n\nThese visuals clarify why Hopf\u2019s fixed symmetry can yield different behavior from flexible clustering.\n\n**Sources:** We drew on core references: Weinberger et al. (2009) on the hashing trick\u30100\u2020L0-L3\u3011, Levy & Goldberg (2014) on PMI/SVD embeddings\u301018\u2020L5-L7\u3011, GloVe (Pennington et al. 2014) and Johnson-Lindenstrauss results on random projection. K-means theory (Lloyd 1982; ref. Scikit-Learn docs\u301013\u2020L3-L8\u3011) explains its adaptive limits. Hopf fibrations are described in topology texts\u301010\u2020L11-L18\u3011. MoE/conditional compute gains are detailed in recent sparse transformer works\u301028\u2020L1-L7\u3011. All above URLs and commit IDs refer to the current project\u2019s repository and literature sources."]}, "status": "finished_successfully", "end_turn": true, "weight": 1.0, "metadata":
